request.question: Are both Dave Evans and Heidi Newfield both singers?

title: Burns & Poe
Trick Pony
List of AC/DC band members
Pour Me (Trick Pony song)
Johnny & June (song)
Heidi Newfield
Cry Cry ('Til the Sun Shines)

sentences: Burns & Poe was an American country music duo composed of Keith Burns and Michelle Poe.
The duo was founded in 2007 after Burns left the group Trick Pony.

Poe was once a solo artist for DreamWorks Nashville, and later played bass guitar for Dierks Bentley and Hank Williams, Jr. The two met in 2007 while Burns was holding auditions to recruit a new lead singer for Trick Pony after Aubrey Collins (who had replaced original lead singer Heidi Newfield just months earlier) left.

Trick Pony is an American country music group, formed in 1996 by Heidi Newfield (lead vocals, acoustic guitar, harmonica), Keith Burns (lead guitar, vocals), and Ira Dean (bass guitar, upright bass, vocals).
They recorded three studio albums: "Trick Pony", "On a Mission", and "R.I.D.E.", released in 2001, 2002, and 2005.
These albums produced eight singles on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart, including four Top 20 hits: "Pour Me", "On a Night like This", "Just What I Do", and "On a Mission".
AC/DC are an Australian hard rock band formed by guitarist brothers Angus and Malcolm Young in 1973.

Originally featuring vocalist Dave Evans, bassist Larry Van Kriedt and drummer Colin Burgess, after a number of lineup changes the band settled with Bon Scott on vocals, Mark Evans on bass guitar and Phil Rudd on drums, and released five albums (two limited to Australia) between 1974 and 1977.

Due to continuing clashes with lead guitarist Angus Young, Evans was later fired from the band shortly after the release of "Let There Be Rock", although "musical differences" was the official reason given for his departure.
Cliff Williams was brought in as his replacement.
"Pour Me" is a debut song recorded by American country music group Trick Pony.
It was released in October 2000 as the first single from their debut album "Trick Pony".
The song was written by group members Keith Burns, Ira Dean and Heidi Newfield with Rory Waters Beighley and Sammy Harp Wedlock.
"Johnny & June" is the solo debut song co-written and recorded by American country music singer Heidi Newfield, the former lead singer of the group Trick Pony.
It was released in March 2008 as the first single from her debut album "What Am I Waiting For", which was released in August 2008 on Curb Records.
The song reached a peak of #11 on the Hot Country Songs charts in late September 2008.
Heidi Newfield (born October 4, 1970) is an American country music artist.
She was lead singer, rhythm guitarist and harmonica player for the group Trick Pony, alongside Keith Burns and Ira Dean from 1996 until 2006, when she left in pursuit of a solo career.
Newfield has begun her solo career on Curb Records, debuting in 2008 with the single, "Johnny & June."

This song, which peaked at No. 11 on the Hot Country Songs charts, is the first release from her solo debut album, "What Am I Waiting For", which has produced a second Top 30 country hit, "Cry Cry ('Til the Sun Shines)."
"Cry Cry ('Til the Sun Shines)" is a song originally recorded by Martina McBride on her 2007 album "Waking Up Laughing".
It was later recorded by American country music artist Heidi Newfield and released in November 2008 as the second single from the album "What Am I Waiting For".
The song reached #29 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs chart.
The song was written by Marv Green, Aimee Mayo, Chris Lindsey and Hillary Lindsey.
Dave Evans (born 20 July 1953) is an Australian singer who briefly sang for the hard rock band AC/DC in 1973–1974.
What Am I Waiting For is the debut solo album from American country music singer Heidi Newfield, formerly the lead vocalist in the group Trick Pony.
It was released on August 5, 2008 on Curb Records and produced by Tony Brown.
The album's lead-off single, "Johnny & June", peaked at number 11 on the Hot Country Songs charts.
This song was followed by "Cry, Cry ('Til the Sun Shines)", which peaked at number 29.
"What Am I Waiting For" debuted on the "Billboard" 200 at number 10 with 34,000 copies sold in its first week.

Stephen "Steve" Nemesh (31 December 1896 in Szécsény, Hungary – 18 September 1975 in San Bernardino, California) was a Hungarian-born open-wheel racecar driver, who emigrated to the United States and competed as an American.
His only American Championship car racing start was the 1926 Indianapolis 500 where he drove an Argyle powered Schmidt chassis.
He started 22nd and was sidelined after completing 41 laps by transmission failure.
He was credited with 21st place.
He drove as a relief driver in the 1927 Indianapolis 500 for Dave Evans.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.

Required output format and constraints:

- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Are both Dave Evans and Heidi Newfield both singers?
hop1_docs: Heidi Newfield | Heidi Newfield (born October 4, 1970) is an American country music artist.  She was lead singer, rhythm guitarist and harmonica player for the group Trick Pony, alongside Keith Burns and Ira Dean from 1996 until 2006, when she left in pursuit of a solo career.  Newfield has begun her solo career on Curb Records, debuting in 2008 with the single, "Johnny & June."  This song, which peaked at No. 11 on the Hot Country Songs charts, is the first release from her solo debut album, "What Am I Waiting For", which has produced a second Top 30 country hit, "Cry Cry ('Til the Sun Shines)."
David Evans (rugby) | David "Dave" Alexander Evans (4 October 1886 – 12 October 1940) was a New Zealand dual-code international who represented his country in both rugby union and rugby league.
Dave Evans (singer) | Dave Evans (born 20 July 1953) is an Australian singer who briefly sang for the hard rock band AC/DC in 1973–1974.

Johnny & June (song) | "Johnny & June" is the solo debut song co-written and recorded by American country music singer Heidi Newfield, the former lead singer of the group Trick Pony.  It was released in March 2008 as the first single from her debut album "What Am I Waiting For", which was released in August 2008 on Curb Records.  The song reached a peak of #11 on the Hot Country Songs charts in late September 2008.

Killing Heidi (album) | Killing Heidi is the third album by Australian rock band Killing Heidi, released by Columbia Records in Australia on 30 August 2004 (see 2004 in music).  It was the band's first album without their producer Paul Kosky—who worked on their previous albums and has a more "sophisticated and grittier" feel to it with the lead singer, Ella Hooper, stating that releasing the album is "just like a re-launch".  "Killing Heidi" debuted in the top ten on the Australian ARIA Albums Chart, making it their second top ten album and sales were similar to those of Killing Heidi's second album, "Present" (2002) – both were certified gold by ARIA for 35000 units shipped.  Their third album gave Killing Heidi another top twenty single with "I Am", but its second single, "Calm Down" was not a major hit.

Rachel Bay Jones | Rachel Bay Jones (born November 8, 1969) is an American stage actress and singer.  She has played the roles of Catherine in the 2013 Broadway revival of "Pippin" and Evan's mother Heidi in "Dear Evan Hansen".  The latter earned her the 2017 Tony Award for Best Featured Actress in a Musical.

Tiffany Evans discography | American singer and songwriter Tiffany Evans has released one studio album, two extended plays/mixtapes, nine singles, two featured singles and five promotional singles. Evans appeared on "Star Search" in February 2003, where she won the Grand Champion title in junior singer division.  She competed against fellow singers Lisa Tucker and David Archuleta, who both later appeared on "American Idol".  In the same year she signed to Columbia Records.

Ambiguities:

  - [2] "David "Dave" Alexander Evans (4 October 1886 – 12 October 1940) ... represented his country in both rugby union and rugby league"

Bridge / missing fact:
- Need to resolve which "Dave Evans" the question refers to (the AC/DC-era Australian singer or the rugby player David "Dave" Alexander Evans). Heidi Newfield's singer status is confirmed in the passages; the identity mapping for "Dave Evans" is unresolved.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page: "Dave Evans (singer)"
- Open page: "David Evans (rugby)"
- Open page or search: "Dave Evans (disambiguation)" or search query "Dave Evans singer AC/DC 1973"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Dave Evans (singer) | Dave Evans (born 20 July 1953) is an Australian singer who briefly sang for the hard rock band AC/DC in 1973–1974.

Dave Evans (bluegrass) | William "Dave" Evans (July 24, 1950 - June 26, 2017) was a tenor singer, banjo player, composer, and bluegrass band leader.  He was noted for his powerful tenor vocal range and for his style which bridged traditional and contemporary bluegrass.  Notable songs written by Evans include "One Loaf of Bread," "Highway 52," "99 Years is Almost for Life," and "Be Proud of the Grey in Your Hair."

Dave Mackay (musician) | Dave Mackay (born 1932) is an American jazz pianist, singer-composer with roots in the works of Art Tatum, Bud Powell, and Bill Evans, who favors the standards of the 1940s and 1950s and the bossa novas of Luíz Eça, Antonio Carlos Jobim, and João Gilberto when performing.
Dave Evans (footballer) | David G. "Dave" Evans (born 20 May 1958) is an English retired professional footballer who made more than 500 league appearances for three clubs.
Dave Evans (reporter) | Dave Evans (born 1962) is an American television journalist.  Since 1999, he has been a reporter for WABC-TV in New York City, New York.

Dave Evans (entrepreneur) | Dave Evans is an entrepreneur who led the design of Apple's first mouse and who co-founded Electronic Arts before becoming a Consulting Assistant Professor at Stanford University.

Rockin' in the Parlour | Rockin' in the Parlour is the B-side of the first single issued by hard rock band AC/DC.  The song was released on 22 July 1974 under the Australian record label Albert Productions.  This single is the only commercial recording of the group's first singer Dave Evans.
response.summary_2: Heidi Newfield is an American country music artist, and Dave Evans is an Australian singer who briefly sang for AC/DC in 1973–1974.
